UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 21 CONSIDERATION OF REQUEST FOR APPROVAL BY PARLIAMENT OF PROTOCOL ON WOMEN AND YOUTH IN TRADE TO THE AGREEMENT ESTABLISHING THE AFRICAN CONTINENTAL FREE TRADE AREA, (AFCFTA) IN TERMS OF SECTION 231(2) OF CONSTITUTION, 1996 CONSIDERATION OF REQUEST FOR APPROVAL BY PARLIAMENT OF PROTOCOL ON COMPETITION TO THE AGREEMENT ESTABLISHING THE AFRICAN CONTINENTAL FREE TRADE AREA, (AFCFTA) IN TERMS OF SECTION 231(2) OF CONSTITUTION, 1996 Mr M C MASINA: House Chairperson, the Portfolio Committee on Trade, Industry and Competition considered the Protocol to the Agreement of Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area on Women and Youth in Trade, and secondly, Competition Policy on 10 and 17 June 2025. The protocols are broadly aimed at establishing fair trade practices and facilitate participation and inclusion within the African Continental Free Trade Area. The Protocol on Women and Youth in Trade aimed at ensuring the inclusion of women and youth in intra-Africa trade. In addition, it seeks to maximize the opportunity presented by AfCFTA to women and youth by addressing some of the challenges UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 22 they specifically face when involved in formal trading such as lack of access to finance, safety concern on borders as well as the protection from harassment. The protocol can be used to expand opportunities in labour intensive sectors as well as sectors with strong rural and women employment, amongst many other things. In terms of the provision of the protocol, it aims at eliminating the non-tariff barriers affecting women and youth in trade. In this regard, there is a need for key government departments to jointly develop strengthening and implement national policies and programmes to target participation of women and youth in trade opportunities offered by the establishment of the AfCFTA. These include the incentive export promotion, the activities and trade-related technical assistance, amongst others, to women and youth. The Protocol on Competition Policy establishes a continental competition regime to enhance the competition, thus facilitating market access, efficiency, inclusion, growth, structural economic transformation, amongst others. It also UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 23 addresses the anticompetitive practices that cannot effectively be regulated at national or regional level such as cross-border carters, mergers and acquisitions and abuse of prominence by large multinational firms in key sectors. It is important to address these competition issues to ensure that companies located within the continent can compete fairly and for new entrants to enter the market and to take advantage of the opportunities within the continent as well as to protect consumers against unfair pricing practices, amongst others. The protocol will set rules regulating the conduct of firms within the AfCFTA and not that of African governments. It specifically excludes competition matters that only affect individual countries, thus it does not encroach on South Africa’s jurisdiction. Rather, it reinforces South Africa’s ability to combat international anticompetitive practice and align to policy objective and the existing national legislation related to competition matters. In terms of section 213(2) of the Constitution the Portfolio Committee on Trade, Industry and Competition recommends that the House approves the Protocol on to the Agreement of UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 24 Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, firstly, on Youth and Women; and secondly, on Competition Policy. I thank you. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 46 The African continent free trade area is different from the current neoliberal paradigm of the GATT in the sense that it integrates the South African economy into the continental market, in a manner that reconciles trade liberalization with the national development goals, including economic growth, job creation, and the sharing of opportunities and rewards of trade liberalization. The sharing of opportunities and the rewards of trade liberalization has received ever more focused attention in the continental free trade area. To be sure, the objectives of trade of the Continental Free Trade Area include among other things: Equal, the maximization of opportunities and benefits, to support and enhance the effective participation of women and youth in intra Africa trade, to strengthen the capacity of an access to opportunities for women and youth in trade, and to improve inclusion of women and youth in trade in the implementation of this free trade area, to achieve sustainable economic development at the national, regional and continental levels. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 47 In this context of South Africa, the objectives of this protocol would not be pursued for their own sake. National government departments, according to the Department of Trade, Industry and Competition in South Africa, DTIC, will coordinate jointly to explore and enhance the implementation of national policies to privilege women and youth. For example, through incentives and export promotion activities and trade related technical assistance for women and youth own enterprises, safety and border posts and so on. While the Continental Free Trade Area unlocks and shapes a viable industrialization path that South Africa can pursue, it must be coordinated within the competition policy. This is important because the Continental Free Trade Area enables transnational companies to operate continentally, and therefore there is a need for competition regulation, so that African countries can be capacitated to regulate the scope and activities of these transnational companies, TNC, as well as their anti-competitive practices. Also building on skills to ensure that we’re able to participate meaningfully within the value chains. Crucially, this protocol reinforces South Africa’s ability to combat international anti-competitive practices and alliance UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 48 to South Africa’s policy objectives and existing national legislation. Moreover, South Africa’s competition policy continues to serve as a role model to other competition legislative frameworks across the continent and the region due to its potential efficiencies. They ANC supports the tabling and adoption of this committee report. I thank you. Debate concluded. Question put in respect of the First Order. Protocol accordingly approved (Freedom Front Plus dissenting). Question put in respect of the Second Order. Protocol accordingly approved. Speaker:
CONSIDERATION OF REPORT OF PORTFOLIO COMMITTEE ON PUBLIC 
SERVICE AND ADMINISTRATION ON UPDATE RELATING TO PETITION OF 
MEMORANDUM BY FORMER CIVIL SERVANTS ON UNPAID PENSIONS AND 
RELATED BENEFITS 
 
Mr J N DE VILLIERS
Speech_Text: Chairperson, the report before the House today relates to a petition of former civil servants from the Civil Servants Pension Redress Movement relating to unpaid pensions and related benefits. During the Sixth Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly received a memorandum of grievances relating to former civil servants of parastatals of the Republic of South Africa, which comprises Transkei, Bophuthatswana, Venda and Ciskei pensioners. The grievances were pertaining to pension monies not paid whilst working for the apartheid government due to unfair discrimination based on race. The Speaker referred the matter to the then chairperson of the committee of the Sixth Parliament for further processing and accountability. The issues were however not resolved by the committee in the Sixth UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 72 Parliament and therefore the new and current committee re- engaged with the petition on 23 October 2024, just after the start of the Seventh Parliament. Our committee could however only partially deal with the matter without key stakeholders like the Government Pension Administration Agency, GPAA, and the Government Employees Pension Fund, GEPF, representatives present in our sitting. Without their crucial input into the matter, our committee was of the view that this petition can only effectively be resolved together with the Finance Committee and the involvement of the GEPF and the GPAA. The committee took a resolution to have a joint committee meeting with the Standing Committee on Finance, where both the GPAA and the GEPF were invited to attend and present on the matter. The joint committee met in March of this year in an effort to bring resolution to the matter and finally deal with the petition of former government employee pensioners. After presentations and inputs from the GEPF and the GPAA, the joint committee made a proposal for the establishment of a task team in the form of an ad hoc committee for pension redress in general. It was further proposed that the task team should be UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 73 gazetted and consult with all affected members in provinces to ensure inclusiveness in the process. Our committee will monitor the work of the GEPF and the GPAA in the conclusion of this matter as it remains important that all qualifying former public service members receive the pension redress that they qualify for. The committee concerns itself with all matters relating to the health, wellbeing and justice for members of the South African Public Service and Administration. These officials choose to serve the state and the people of this country, and it is of utmost importance that they do this knowing that their pension fund - the GEPF, is being well administered so that adequate and responsible provision is made for their retirement after their service to the state. It is therefore with great concern that I have received a letter regarding the squandering of state employee savings entrusted to the GEPF and specifically via the Public Investment Corporation, PIC, the asset manager’s investment and their shielding in Daybreak Foods. Daybreak Foods was initially purchased by the PIC for R1,19 billion with an additional R215 million, a quarter billion rand more loans granted in the last year. A third of the shareholding in UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 74 Daybreak Foods is held by the GEPF. Despite this massive investment of close to R1,5 billion, Daybreak Foods is currently in business rescue, placing serious doubt on the PIC’s ability to recover any of the government employment savings that have been invested into it. In recent years, the PIC has appointed a series of boards and executives, dogged by corruption and mismanagement allegations, to Daybreak Foods and at least one forensic investigation implicates the company secretary of the PIC in irregular appointments. In addition, it has now come to light that up to 44 of the companies that the PIC has invested in are the focus of the PIC’s turnaround and value-added department - an indication that Daybreak may be one of many investments using government employee savings that are either in business rescue or struggle to meet their debt obligations. In light of this, as chairperson, I have requested a joint sitting with the Finance Committee, specifically to call PIC senior staff, including the chief investment officer and the company secretary, before our committees to account for, the highly questionable use of state employee funds in both initial investments and subsequent loans to Daybreak Foods, the lack of responsible shareholder oversight of Daybreak Foods and other unlisted entities, including the appointment UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 75 of boards and CEOs in these entities, to disclose the names of the companies currently in the turnaround and value-added department, as well as an overview of their financial position, and to make available the forensic investigation. We will also request that the chairperson of Daybreak Foods and the business rescue practitioner appear in front of the committee. The PIC and the GEPF use and lose public servants’ pension money to dabble in high-risk investment, and it cannot continue. Declarations of vote: Ms L R POTGIETER: Hon Chairperson, the report before us today represents three decades of government attempts to reconcile pension redress for government employees who served in our schools, our hospitals and our public institutions prior to 1994, many of whom did not live long enough to enjoy what should have been a token of appreciation for their years spent in service. This report is a result of the President having to reopen the entire process and form another task team due to claims of exclusive practices in the original determination. While we welcome the report from the Government Pensions Administration Agency, which states that 99,3% of qualifying pensioners have now been paid, we cannot hide behind UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 76 statistics if even one qualifying candidate has been left stranded. In our joint meeting, some organisations clearly articulated their frustration in being excluded from consultation and shut out of the national task team. Speech_Text: Chairperson, hon members, the unrelenting scourge of gang violence continues to devastate communities in the northern areas of Nelson Mandela Bay. Since 2019, over 1 000 people have been brutally murdered in that specific part of the city. More than 200 in just the last two years, with some victims as young as three years old, and despite years of plans, promises, and so-called interventions, the killings continue unabated. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 90 The portfolio committee’s oversight and the petition before having laid bare the failings of the SA Police Service and the National Prosecuting Authority, NPA, in addressing the specific crisis effectively. The response from the SAPS in Nelson Mandela Bay has been marked by inadequate planning, poor co-ordination, and a chronic inability to disrupt the organised syndicates behind the violence that I speak of. And for far too long, the SA Police Services management has hidden behind the excuse of limited resources. But let me say it plainly, this crisis will not be solved by throwing more money at it. No number of resources can overcome the failure of integrity. To illustrate the depth of this rot, consider this disturbing example: a detective in that very area admitted to having committed an armed robbery using an illicit firearm in the very region he was meant to protect. Any sanction? Two months of unpaid suspension, and now he is back at work to protect and serve. You cannot run an intelligence-led prosecution-driven policing model if the intelligence is compromised at the top. You cannot expect frontline officers to stand up to gangsters if they have no confidence in their leadership. They lack UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 91 confidence in their leadership’s integrity and whether they are competent enough to stand up against organised crime. The recent chaos and the instability within the SA Police Service management, especially in recent weeks, speak volumes about the fractured and ineffective approach used by our current leadership. We have seen in Nelson Mandela Bay how fractured, politicised, and ineffective command structures have become, and this enables gangs to flourish and ordinary citizens to live in fear. The recent public allegations by Lieutenant General Mkhwanazi about political interference and criminal syndicates' infiltration in the SA Police Service at senior levels only confirm that communities already ... ... They cannot trust the SA Police Service to prioritise ... ... their safety ahead of self-interest ... The HOUSE CHAIRPERSON (Mr W Horn): Hon Cameron, hold on. Hon members on the virtual platform, please ensure that you are muted until being recognised. In the House, similarly, please remain muted until you are recognised. Hon Cameron, please ... Mr I CAMERON: It is not surprising that we sit here today with what seems like we have ... UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 92 The HOUSE CHAIRPERSON (Mr W Horn): Hon Cameron, hold on. Hon Mkhaliphi. Ms H O MKHALIPHI: On a point of order. It cannot be, Chair, that the ANC does not have a declaration of important matters. Now they are disturbing the House. The HOUSE CHAIRPERSON (Mr W Horn): Hon member ... hon Cameron, my apologies. Please proceed. Mr I CAMERON: Thank you, Chair. It is no surprise, then, that we sit here today with what feels like three Ministers of Police. We have one on special leave, another acting, and another one acting for the acting, and yet no clear decisive leadership taking responsibility. Hon members, this is a wake- up call. This is not the time for more platitudes about community partnerships or talks about integrated approaches. Those words are hollow if the leadership itself cannot be trusted. In Nelson Mandela Bay and the entire SA Police Service desperately needs credible, urgent integrity intervention. Firstly, independent vetting of all senior officers; secondly, mandatory lifestyle audits; and lastly, immediate removal of UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 93 compromised commanders and managers. Once this integrity intervention is completed, the SA Police Service must fully commit to the policing model our country so desperately needs. Intelligence-led and prosecution-driven investigations that focus on dismantling the criminal syndicates behind the violence and delivering successful convictions. If integrity is the cornerstone of policing, then ours is significantly cracked. The people of Nelson Mandela Bay have suffered enough, and they deserve more than just excuses. They deserve a Police Service they can trust, one that dares to dismantle both street gangs and syndicates within its ranks. It is time we reform the SA Police Service from the top down, with integrity, accountability, and an unwavering commitment to justice, and return to a policing model that genuinely protects and serves our communities - one that is intelligence-led and prosecution-driven. As chair of the Portfolio Committee on Police, I table this report and move for its adoption. I thank you.

English: UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 132 It is a great privilege to be able to table this Eskom Debt Relief Amendment Bill. It takes place while within a context where Eskom used to be a site for state capture. Where in fact for many instances we used to live in a world of loadshedding whilst many South Africans still face load reduction. But none of us members can deny the fact that the crippling years of load shedding, the closing of factories, the hollowing out of townships and suffocating small businesses is something that we are fast moving away from. Eskom indeed can become that energy provider that is able to demonstrate to the people of South Africa that the reforms that we make are able to in fact turn a corner. The Eskom Debt Relief Amendment Bill is more than just a funding instrument, it is indeed a commitment to recovery, to structured oversight and irreversible reform. And as Parliament, we must continue to act as constitutional guardians of public money, ensuring that no cent is spent without consequence or no intervention is made without reform. I want to affirm that there has been incredible progress since 2023 when this Act was put forward. The original Eskom debt in 2023 committed R254 billion over three years but with fairly strict conditions that there would be no new borrowing, no UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 133 misuse of funds and a focus on transmission and distribution investment. I want to affirm that as Eskom has carried the work, revenue has increased by 16%, diesel spending has decreased. Ultimately, profit before tax has reached R34 billion and the debt at Eskom is something that is starting to decline when you look at their cash revenues. These gains are not accidental. They take an incredible leadership and a team that is committed at Eskom. So, when we look at this Bill, hon members, our consideration is the fact that the allocation that was done for 2025-26 be adjusted from R40 billion to R80,2 billion and introduce R10 billion additional in 2028-29. It effectively means that Eskom will be sending less money back and ultimately demonstrating the fact that it is beginning to manage its own finances. This is an important issue. It demonstrates that this is not about technicalities, it is about us hardening our fiscal responsibilities. I do want to caution in the better space of financial space that Eskom is in, let's not deny the fact that municipalities still pose a significant risk to Eskom. Municipal debt has now moved to R94,6 billion, with cities like Johannesburg and Tshwane failing to be able to fulfil their financial UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 134 obligations. As a portfolio committee, this matter was debated deeply. Some of the issues that we want to raise is the fact that Eskom needs to explore prepaid bulk electricity supply. It needs to ensure that Distribution Agency Agreement, DAA, agreements are entered into, and we need to ensure that Eskom's financial viability is not crippled by municipalities. We are also clear that as we table this Eskom Debt Amendment Bill, the unbundling of Eskom into generation, transmission and distribution, the three entities must be accelerated and must be put forward in transparency so that members of South Africa are able to get a clear and enforceable unbundling timeline so we understand how the assets will be split going forward. That equally so, Eskom needs to be committed to a process of the Just Energy Transition, ensuring energy moves - not just to green - but also equitable job creating and developmental driven objectives. As a committee we would like to make a number of recommendations: That at first Treasury and the Department of Electricity monitor and enforce compliance with all conditions under the 2023 Act; that Eskom submit quarterly reports to this Parliament as they usually do; that clarity be provided on how loan to equity conversion will be handled; that Eskom UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 135 table a clear plan, costed plan and financing for the Just Energy Transition, and where some of the conditions that were put forward such as selling non-core assets be fulfilled, certainly before the maturation of the debt. So, hon members, as I bring this input to a close, this is not just about another financing Bill, it is about the early signs of improving Eskom. It is about the beginnings of saying to ourselves, can we then take energy supply in South Africa and make it something available to all citizens. That no citizen will live in the township and ultimately not have electricity. And that we keep a grid that is stable. As Chair of Standing Committee on Public Accounts, Scopa, I would like to clearly set out that our job is not just to bail out Eskom, but to hold it to account to make sure it gets better, to make sure energy is secure, and therefore I commit this report to be adopted by this House. The main purpose of the Eskom Debt Relief Amendment Bill is the restructuring of the remaining financial support to better UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 190 align with Eskom’s debt maturing profile. This adjustment has reduced the total debt relief package from R254 billion to R230 billion. As of July, Eskom has received R140 billion in shareholder loans under the Eskom Debt Relief Act, Act 7 of 2023 to support its financial recovery and operational stability. The Minister of Finance has approved the conversion of the R140 billion into equity, based on Eskom’s demonstrated compliance with strategic conditions attached to the debt relief. Interest payments to date amount to R1,2 billion. The debt relief has delivered significant benefits in supporting Eskom’s operational recovery and addressing the persistent load shedding challenges. The key outcomes of this have been the easing of liquidity pressures, enabling Eskom to redirect internal cashflows to critical operational and infrastructure needs, contributing to improved plant performance and reduced load shedding. The second is intended funding to support generation plant recovery and maintenance, improving operational reliability and energy availability. To safeguard against future unsustainable debt, strict conditions remain in place, including borrowing restrictions — which cannot happen without National Treasury approval — enhanced financial controls, progress on unbundling, and acceleration of generation recovery. The debt relief framework does link financial support to tangible governance and structural reforms. This reflects the robust oversight and controls implemented by government to monitor the effective utilisation of the debt relief and to enable Eskom’s continued compliance. This means that this Bill positively contributed to restoring generation capacity through dealing with debt liability, which enabled increased revenue to be utilised for maintenance. This ensured an improvement in the energy availability factor from 48% to 68%, and Eskom is intending further improvements of the energy availability factor to 70%. This signalled a reduction in UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 192 unplanned shutdowns and improved plant performance with an increase in generation capacity. The people and the economy are starting to feel the positive contribution which this Bill has made, as load shedding reached a peak in 2023 of some 333 days. Since the introduction of this Bill, there have been more than 300 days without load shedding, coupled with a reduction in the usage of diesel at peaking plants. This is evidence of a plan, coupled with the correct legislation and regulation, that is working. Our aim is not merely to end load shedding but to ensure that generation capacity is increased to match the increasing demand and ensure inclusive economic growth and job creation. Our aim in reducing the debt liability through this Bill is to ensure the increase in generation capacity to enable the implementation of the Medium-Term Development Plan. Our aim through this Bill is also to ensure that the Integrated National Electrification Plan, which has been a success story since 1994, not only delivers connections to historically disadvantaged communities but ensures that it is accompanied by electricity supply at the cheapest possible cost. Speaker: The DEPUTY MINISTER OF FINANCE (Dr D Masondo)
Speech_Text: Hon Deputy Speaker, hon members and hon Molefe, citing one cause of a phenomenon, a problem in this case the Eskom debt, in the midst of many causes, does not help in telling the entire truth. Part of the problem of the debt at Eskom is due to what you know better than me, the cost overruns in Medupi and Kusile, as well as the shift from the cost-plus power stations, which generated a lot of costs in so far as generating energy is concerned. Periodisation of history is not neutral. You can cite problems that started in 1994 and apportion everything on the ANC. As hon Lekganyane has said, ANC has owned up the good and the bad. You can focus on the pre 1994 problems to hide the current challenges and problems that we are facing. Hon members, what this debate shows is that had we not provided the debt relief two years ago to Eskom, the rolling blackouts would have possibly continued, and our economy would have significantly deteriorated, not only because of the power cuts, but due also to the rising sovereign risk premium, as UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 194 other speakers have said. Because of this debt relief and other interventions, today loadshedding is lower than it has been in the recent past. Loadshedding is an exception and no longer the rule. We agree, hon Hlazo-Webster, that we have not fully solved our challenges, but we have made significant progress. The debt relief programme has provided Eskom within an enabling balance sheet to spend more money in improving Eskom’s capacity to supply electricity. This is not just about keeping the lights on; this is about protecting jobs and recovering our economy. The Bill before us also shows that when we give tough love to our state-owned entities, SOEs, as our Finance Minister would say, sticking to plans rather than opting for quick fixes, we realise better results. Hon member, unlike in the past where we came before you to ask this House to approve more funds to bailout Eskom, today we are requesting the House to reduce Eskom’s burden on the fiscus. REVENUE LAWS AMENDMENT BILL (First Reading debate) UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 198 Dr M J MASWANGANYI: Deputy Speaker, Ministers, hon members, I hereby present the report on the Committee on the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill. Nevertheless, concerns were raised regarding the implementation of this legislation. Consequently, the Minister of Finance introduced the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill to address the concerns raised by workers Pension Fund Administrators, Sars and the National Treasury. The 2025 Revenue Laws Amendment Bill introduces the following key changes: Firstly, clarification on the timing of the seeding amount calculation providing certainty to retirement fund members and administrators. Secondly, optionality for retirement funds to determine the calculation date for seeding amount either 31 August 2024 or the last day of the election month as per fund rules and communication to members for those UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 199 eligible to opt into the two-port system. Thirdly, clarification of the definition of members interest in each of the three components, specifying that the components must be reduced proportionally by applicable section 37-D deductions. Fourthly, alignment of the definition of retirement annuity fund to ensure consistency with the intended objectives of the reform. Lastly, alignment of the definition of retirement annuity fund to ensure consistency with the intended objectives of the reform. Deputy Speaker, let us commend the significant progress made by the ANC government in implementing the two-port pension reforms since September 2024. For the first time, workers have the opportunity to access a portion of their savings without needing to resign from their jobs or withdraw their entire funds. We are encouraged by the positive response from workers to these progressive reforms, with millions submitting applications for relief from their pension and provident funds. The two-port pension reforms and the 10% increase up to R30 000 have provided relief to millions of workers offering access to a third of their future savings when needed. As with transformational changes aimed at improving the lives of millions, there will be challenges and no shortage of confusion from right wingers. This will dissipate as reforms reach millions of struggling and indebted workers. It is our responsibility as representatives of the people to ensure that worker’s needs are addressed. While we acknowledge and celebrate these achievements, we are infuriated by the revelation that over 7 700 employers have failed to pay their workers pension contributions during this relief process. The Financial Sector Conduct Authority, FSCA, UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 201 must take firm and decisive action against this delinquent employers. Deputy Speaker, the committee reaffirms the importance of inclusive public participation and commends stakeholders, including individual citizens for engaging in the legislative process. However, the committee knows that submissions, not referencing specific clauses are difficult to incorporate into technical deliberations. It therefore recommends that Parliament improve its public participation mechanisms, such as using plain language summaries of bills, submissions templates and orientation sessions to help stakeholders prepare clause linked inputs. Public hearings were held on the 1 July 2025, with submissions received from various stakeholders. On 8 July 2025, National Treasury and Sars presented a response document to the committee which outline all public comments and deliberations by the committee on the Bill. The public submissions on the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill reflect a wide array of technical and policy related concerns raised by stakeholders. The committee knows that stakeholders express broad support for the two-port retirement system UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 202 implemented on the 1 September 2024, citing its potential to improve retirement outcomes and liquidity access for members. However, stakeholders emphasized that the effectiveness of the system depends on resolving certain technical issues. The committee notes stakeholder concern regarding the restriction in paragraph 6B of the Second Schedule of this Bill, which limits fund members to one is to one transfer on withdrawal. This change removes the pre-existing option to proportionally split transfers across multiple funds, thereby potentially affecting member flexibility and unitization options. National Treasury indicated that this provision will be reviewed in the next legislative cycle and justify the restriction as a safeguard against premature commutation. The committee considers the matter to be a substantive and recommends that it be reviewed with urgency in consultation with stakeholders. Concerns were raised regarding the inconsistency of the use of terms nominee, dependent and beneficiary across the Income Tax Act and the Pension Funds Act. While National Treasury declined to replace nominee and dependent with beneficiary, the committee supports a comprehensive review in subsequent UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 203 legislation to ensure legal coherence and administrative certainty across statutory regimes governing retirement funds. The committee notes the inconsistencies in the effective dates between the 2024 Taxation Laws Amendment Act and the 2024 Revenue Laws Amendment Act. Although Sars began implementation from 1 September 2024, National Treasury confirmed that 1 March 2025 remains the applicable date. The committee accepted this clarification but recommends that Treasury and Sars improve intra-bill coordination to avoid confusion in future legislative process. The committee processed the Bill clause by clause considered and adopted this progressive Bill. English: This Bill speaks to a South African. This is a South African who has been working for much of their lives and now they find themselves in difficult financial positions. Hon members, let us recognise that the conditions of our country are such that for too many citizens they find themselves without work, they find themselves living in high levels of poverty with more than half our citizens living below the upper poverty line, they are confronting an economy that is growing at a very stagnant rate at less than 1% in the last decade, they are living in conditions - as the SA Local Government Association Salga, describes them - that many household owners are incapable to pay even municipalities this is the person we are talking to. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 226 I would like to recommend, hon members, as we deal with this Bill today that we don’t deal with it technically, but we deal with answering the question: What is the dignity of this person that we are trying to give to. At first, Deputy Minister and both Deputy Ministers here, I think you will agree with me that it is high time now that we think hard about how do we help South Africans understand their financial conditions both in the short-term and in the long-term. I think financial literacy and financial understanding must be as paramount as knowing your HIV status. It will help you understand your financial transactions over a long period of time. We need to be able to put that in place when you read reports about what South Africans end up doing with some of the withdrawals they make. Secondly, BOSA wholeheartedly endorses the view of the two-pot system. We are clear about the fact that it is a way for citizens to be able to get short-term relief. However, having said that, I think we need to think hard about how we tax the process of this relief. It is common course that as people make withdrawals they become shocked to discover that 36% of emergency withdrawals are highly taxed. So, they find themselves in this crisis where they are withdrawing and getting significant taxation that comes with it. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 227 Chairperson, whilst this might be part of the process of doing this but what we cannot do as a government we need to recognise that people are withdrawing in the place of emergency. We cannot say to people that we care about your crisis and then tax the relief like it is the same as the luxury. We support this Bill. We urge that we use plain language and explaining it to our citizens. As I have spoken earlier, we would like to make it clear that as BOSA we think that there should be much more digital accessibility for the public, especially young income earners, to learn about retirement policy, et cetera. In conclusion, hon members, let us be clear, this is a withdrawal that workers use as defence against poverty. So, as we support this thing, we are saying, let us make it fair taxation on withdrawal, let us give transparent timelines for future corrections, let us make a retirement regime that restores public trust and financial dignity and let us ensure that for many South Africans - as they know what their financial status is - this is not just a mere reform. It is imperative to restore the dignity of all South Africans. Setswana: Ke a leboga. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 228 Dr M J BURKE: Mr Chair, much of the content of my address today has been covered by speakers from the IFP, the PA, Rise, and even ASA and I wish I could tell the House that the Revenue Laws Amendment Act is interesting, exciting, and vital to our country’s future. But unfortunately, it’s boring enough to put an accountant to sleep. While I want to be clear that the DA supports this legislation, there’s something sad about us needing it in the first place. In essence, this Revenue Laws Amendment Bill is an attempt at fixing technical mistakes in the so-called two-pot reforms to our retirement system. You’ll also remember, Mr Chair, that this retirement reform is in turn a response to the financial desperation haunting our country. Even for those with jobs, disposable income is a myth. Meaning that when emergencies strike, many have no rainy-day fund, and based on a decade of nearly zero economic growth, there is now zero hope for many people that they will be able to put aside savings for such emergencies. So, we’ve ended here with a stopgap solution. The two-pot system, which this bill aims to reinforce, is akin to the cadre class in South Africa, barely noting the gaping hole created by the iceberg of corruption which is rapidly sinking our country. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 229 Looking up just enough from the clinking of cadre cocktails to throw us a life jacket. The equivalent of letting them eat cake - or should I say - give them two pots. You see, we in the DA are not against life jackets. It’s just that we’re aware of the cold water. What does it say about the desperation of our country that we’ve had four million withdrawals in less than a year, with half a million of Africans dipping into their pots a second time? Our GDP growth for the start of 2025 is 0,1% or 10 basis points. Which begs the question, Mr Chair, if South Africans didn’t tap into their futures to pay for their present, would we now not be in a recession? That brings me to today’s bill. This legislation is basically the National Treasury saying, “whoops, sorry, there's a few little mistakes, little imperfections in the previous approach.” Not to worry, though. Here’s a patch that will fix those exclusions. Except it seems that the patch itself has problems. National Treasury has already told the Finance Committee that the law we’re reading in front of this House today itself will need fixing on as many as three issues. The life jacket more or less works, but this patch will need a patch. It will require more time from Parliament’s Finance Committee, which has no shortage of pressing oversight that it needs to conduct. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 230 We surely have enough other matters, including a comprehensive expenditure review, to focus on. On that point, I’d like to ask the absent Minister of Finance and at the risk ...

Speaker: The MINISTER OF FINANCE
Speech_Text: I am not absent. The HOUSE CHAIRPERSON (Mr W Horn): Order, hon members! Hon Burke, you stand corrected. Please proceed. Dr M J BURKE: I stand corrected, Mr Chair. The virtually attending Minister of Finance, at the risk of firing shots, like the ANC seems to be firing the executive. Could you please, Mr Minister, when you next address this House, use your speaking time to tell us if - in between finding funds for the national monologue - you’ve actually appointed the agreed to person to head the spending review. Has that happened? So, Mr Chair, our country stumbles trapped inside an economic and ethical crisis. Yet here we are debating the bill that itself needs fixing. Our pots are not full and running over. They are as hollow as the words of many of our politicians. We live in a country where we now need two pots, and unfortunately, both of them are empty. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 231 Mr M K B MOLEFE: House Chairperson, let’s call a spade a spade. The problem in South Africa is that you have the majority of the people who were oppressed by an erstwhile crime against humanity that has been declared by the financial sector to be high credit risk. Credit is not available to them. The economy can’t grow without credit. The economy can grow without small businesspeople being able to borrow money to start another business. Small businesspeople are being able to borrow money to start their business, especially in a country that has 41% unemployment. The only hope is that those people will somehow get credit and start financing some economic activity, but that is not available. The reason that is not available - let’s call a spade a spade - because our financial sector is not transformed. The reason the credit is not available is that the structure of the South African economy is still the structure of the apartheid economy, where black people live in Gugulethu and are high risk, and white people live in Bishopscourt and are low risk. Black people have got money in the banking system, but that money is not available for them to get as credit. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 232 So, what do we do as this august House? As the National Treasury, we say those of you that want credit - go and withdraw your own pension so that you can start the business. No, it doesn’t work like that. We need to confront ... to take the bull by the horns and say we need bigger access to credit. What has happened with this two-pot system is an indication precisely of that problem of the absence of credit or the unavailability of credit. We cannot take people’s future consumption spending today. They take their future consumption spending that is in their pension and allow them or encourage them to use it today. What’s going to happen when they get old? What’s going to happen when they get old and they don’t have pensions because they used it in the two-pot system which later on we are going to patch again and have another technical fix to make it a three-port system, and so on and so forth. Rather than saying, leave your pension alone, go and get credit. Go and get credit and do whatever you want to do. That is the fundamental problem of our problem in South Africa. We need to take on the financial sector and say that please, you have to transform and start providing more credit. Hon House Chairperson, there is another crime that has been committed and that is the employers that are not paying over the pension fund money of their employees to the pension UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 233 funds. That issue came to Parliament, and it has been reported to the police. We’ve even asked through the written question in this House to Minister of Police as to: How many employers have been convicted or even charged for not having paid pension fund money? Because our law makes it a criminal offence not to pay over the pension fund money. The Minister of Police Senzo Mchunu responded to our written question and said no convictions have been recorded. So, I challenge hon Maswanganyi that we should revisit this issue. We should take up this issue of the employers that are not paying over people’s pensions to the pension funds, and make sure that ... hon Gwede Mantashe ensures that police investigate this maladministration and that we do get the conviction of those employers that have not paid over their pension fund contributions to the pension funds. Thank you, Chairperson. Mr S C SEKOATI: House Chair, hon Deputy Ministers in the House, the Chief Whip of the Largest Party, members of this august House, let me start off by correcting the fallacy stated here, particularly by my colleagues. Firstly, the powers and functions of the Minister of Finance are actually established by the Constitution, Chapter 13 of the UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 234 Constitution of 1996, adopted in May, and amended in October 1996. So, it is not an apartheid created Constitution. It is not. It is the powers that are derived from this Constitution that was adopted in 1996. No, just to clarify the fallacy because it looks like you do not understand this Constitution and where the powers are coming from. It is important to make it a base for you to start speaking about the powers and functions of the Minister of Finance. The HOUSE CHAIRPERSON (Mr W Horn): Order, hon members. Mr S C SEKOATI: The second fallacy is in terms of the role that is played by the two-pot system because it is important to understand why the stakeholders came to Parliament and to make sure that this two-pot system is actually implemented. The ANC supported it, but it was because of the debt burdened workers who came and said it would be important for us to have access to the money. So, they are actually loaning themselves the money because of the interests that they are going to be paying. Just to clarify for you. Unfortunately, some of those workers who took their pensions to some of these financial institutions, some took to the building society in Venda, and some proclaimed saviours of these people helped themselves with those savings. I am just trying to clarify that there are UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 235 those self-proclaimed saviours who know what happened to those savings of those people. Having said that, it is very clear that South Africa is one of the countries with an effective retirement system which covers over 16 million pension fund members, with the private sector having over 12 million members. Our history which we all know of colonialism and apartheid, excluded the black majority from the real and true benefits of the pension fund system. Today, as we speak, we have one of the deepened and matured pension fund system which covers all workers of South Africa. This is a significant gain by the ANC democratic government that has restored the dignity of the workers through a comprehensive social security system that provides social protection for workers who in the past sold their labour only to retire without adequate pension fund, if any. Since our democratic government led by the ANC, we have increased the depth of our capital markets as pension funds invest the resources in the South African economy and abroad. The Government Pension Fund, is one of the largest pension fund schemes in the world, enhancing our national savings as a country. We must always bear in mind that the pension fund assets managed by the Public Investment Corporation, which are UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 236 currently above R2,6 trillion are a product of the sweat and toil of our workers, particularly in the Public Service. It is therefore incumbent that these assets should be contributing to the overall wellbeing of workers through supporting the growth of industries and capitalism firms that require investment to protect jobs. The South African labour movement has advocated for the principle of leveraging the pension fund savings to cushion workers from the high cost of living and for their livelihood purposes. The two-pot retirement system has also demonstrated that reforms of the pension which enables a withdrawal do not negatively impair the sustainability of pension savings. We must therefore recall that many workers, when faced with various social and economic challenges, have also resorted to withdrawing their pension fund benefits. This places such workers in a precarious position, which results in the state bearing the cost of providing an old age grant. As such, retired workers lack adequate pensions, if any at that point. The introduction of the two-pot system cushioned the impact of the cost of living for many workers who use the fund to reduce their debts and also respond to various household needs. We know this is not sufficient to resolve all the problems of the UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 237 workers, but it is a significant measure by a caring ANC government through the calls by Progressive Unions and Labour Federation, Cosatu. I hope we do understand that. We must further develop other financial mechanisms that enabled workers to liberate their savings for the financial product through a guarantee. For example, the Pension Funds Act sections 19(5)(a) and 37D allows for an alternative form of housing finance where the loan is secured by your retirement fund savings instead mortgage bond. This can be expanded to other critical aspects of life, such as student loans, commercial investment loans and other financial instruments so much needed. The ANC has always advanced the objective of redistributive measures in order to improve the living conditions of all South Africans. A further focus on other elements that need to be enhanced to enable the two-pot system to be effective and optimal in its benefit for workers is important. This requires a focus on administrative charges that the pension fund charged to its members which have relatively generated a lot of revenue for pension fund administrators and the level of tax applied to the two-pot withdrawal which is currently at the normal tax rate of the workers, which is significant. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 238 A consideration should be made on easing the level of taxation as this reform serves as a cushion for workers and also has a direct positive impact on tax revenue as seen in the 2025 budget. This should be considered in a balanced manner in the interests of the workers. We also call on all workers to be circumspect of the consideration of a two-pot withdrawal as it does impact on the overall pension savings which are critical for the retirement period. This requires workers to always take a balanced approach based on their financial planning and financial resources. One of the negative impacts of the local government that is not financially sustainable is the reality that the default in various financial obligations, including the employer contributions for their pension funds, endanger the livelihoods of workers as it risks workers losing their benefits. The circumstances are noncompliance with section 13 (a) of the Pension Fund Act that requires a notwithstanding any provisions in the rules of a registered fund to the contrary, the employer of any member of such a fund shall pay agreed amount to the fund in full. The review of the White Paper on Local Government is an important process that the ANC has advanced after the UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 239 recognition of structural factors impacting the financial sustainability of municipalities. In other circumstances, financial mismanagement in municipalities leads to some municipalities defaulting due to poor prioritization and fiscal leaks. We need to develop mechanisms that ring-fenced critical obligations from defaults as they have an adverse impact on the livelihoods of municipal workers. The Pension Fund Adjudicator as a statutory ombud scheme founded in 1998 to resolve pension funds related complaints in a procedurally fair, economical and expeditious manner should heighten its work. The Pension Fund Adjudicator received a total of 2349 enquiries on the two-pot system and 239 complaints, with 163 matters resolved and the need to therefore continue to resolve those issues. The relatively low levels of complaints reflect that the two-pot system was effectively implemented by various pension funds. The private sector is also a major contributor of noncompliance with section 13(a) particularly companies in the security sector, which is also constituted by a high number of low-income earners. This requires a sectoral focus to address specific limitations in the sector and resolve the precarious conditions of security guards who work under risky and fragile conditions. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 240 The ANC supports the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill which is more of a technical Bill to enhance the administration of the two- pot system. This includes aspects that relate to seeding date and ensure defined application by respective funds and changes in definitions of the components of the system and to introduce a proportional reduction of the components of the fund in the circumstances of a withdrawal. The ANC will continue to advance measures to transform the financial sector to work for the nation and serve as a source of creation of an equitable society. And as we continue to strengthen our system and transform the financial sector, the National Treasury ought to increase its developmental focus on private pension funds to optimise the social and economic impact as reflected by the role of the PIC. Therefore, the ANC supports the Bill. Setswana: Ke a leboga.

Speaker: The DEPUTY MINISTER OF FINANCE (Mr A N Sarupen)
Speech_Text: House Chair, the Chief Whip of the MKP has asked me to be brief, so I will be as quick as I can. I think it is important that we understand that this Bill has a fair amount of history to it UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 241 and where it comes from. It is not a vacuum, and it was not just put together last year. It was former Minister Mboweni, I think, during his final MTBPS, that floated the idea of creating this in the context of Covid-19, when household savings were entirely depleted and we needed to enable people to be able to access emergency funding. So, the system was the system was launched in September 2024, and it has had an impact on the economy and the people of our country, so far, because it is revealed that there is a demand for this kind of reform. It is clear that we needed this flexibility to prevent workers from quitting their jobs, because a mere 9% of retirement fund members choose to preserve their retirement fund savings when they change jobs. We have got to change that. So, the two-parts reform has prevented countless workers from resigning just to access their pension funds. This in turn has helped provide necessary financial support for them to respond to arising emergencies for financial pressures. By mandating that part of savings must be preserved for retirement, we are also trying to provide financial security and stability as individuals reach retirement age. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 242 I think hon Maimane made the point strongly about financial literacy. That is why it is important that we ensure that there is sufficient preservation in the system so that people don’t deplete their financial savings for retirement in their entirety. So, we seek to balance short-term financial flexibility with long-term retirement security. We have allowed families facing financial emergencies to now have dignified options rather than taking on expensive and destructive household debts. This came about because, unfortunately, in this post-Covid-19 environment, for too many Sout Africans, their only savings account is their pension fund and, in the environment of low growth, this is obviously deeply troubling. Now, what are we trying to achieve with these amendments? Firstly, we are fixing the dates confusion for older members. The government became aware that several funds drafted their rules to reference 31 August 2024 and communicate to the state to their members, members of Provident Funds who were 55 or older on one March 2021 were caught in some administrative confusion. Some funds calculated their seeding amounts based on 31 August and other used the date that they opted in and this created uncertainty and inconsistency. UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 243 The proposed amendments provide a clear solution by providing flexibility, allowing both approaches to be valid. If your fund calculated on 31 August 2024, and you acted on that information, it stands. If your fund used the opt-in date, that is also valid. And why does this matter? For one, it means that fund members or administrators acting in good faith are now also unduly penalised. By providing legal certainty, we want to ensure that everyone is protected - the members wanting to make withdrawals as well as administrators and managers of pension funds that want to stick to the letter of the law. Secondly, we want to ensure that there are fair maintenance deductions. One of the aims for the proposed amendments is to clarify the law under which the two-part system operates. When maintenance orders are deducted from pension fund benefits, under section 37(d) of the Pension Fund Act, the law was not clear as to how this should work across the three components of the two-part system. Our solution is to allow for maintenance reductions be applied proportionally across all three parts - the vested savings and retirement components. This ensures fairness and consistency UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 244 to their children and former spouses depending on maintenance payments can get clarity and certainty. No one should be disadvantaged because of these technical and legal gaps. Thirdly, we are aligning technical references to make sure that while there are some minor but important discrepancies that existed between the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill and the Taxation Laws Amendment Bill, regarding the same provisions, we align these two Bills to prevent confusion or legal challenges, because legal coherence protects fund members from costly disputes and delays. There were some various issues raised in the debate, and I want to respond to some of them. The first is on firms not paying over their pension fund contributions. This must be dealt with: The rule of law must run its course! There was a criticism against the Treasury and the Minister of Finance, particularly in relation to municipalities not paying their funds, and an accusation that the Minister is doing nothing. This is not true. The Minister got a report on 18 municipalities that have not been paying their pension contributions and has invoked section 382(2)(c) of the Municipal Finance Management Act and Section 216(2) of the UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 245 Constitution. Of course, when we invoke these matters, then the Minister gets criticised for going after municipalities, but quite rightly, it is to make sure that they pay over their pension fund contributions. This Bill was never intended as a substitute for growth. It was, as I said, proposed during the Covid-19 era to help people access emergency funding as household savings were depleted during that pandemic era. It is for catastrophic emergencies so that people can withdraw funding without destroying their retirement savings. So, we implemented this reform. We have been monitoring its impact. We are listening to stakeholders, and we are making adjustments to ensure that it works fairly and more efficiently. And yes, we are patching and some of the patches will need patches, but that is the iterative nature of doing something the country has never done before. The truth is that this has delivered some measurable gains. Given the trade-offs inherent to any policy reform, it provides a solution to a real issue that South Africans faced. The initial withdrawals of up to R40 billion provided relief to millions of households without destabilising financial UNREVISED HANSARD NATIONAL ASSEMBLY TUESDAY, 22 JULY 2025 Page: 246 markets and the broader pension fund landscape. This reform has allowed a form of debt relief that steers those in need of short-term cash away from high-interest debts and loan sharks and other things out there, while in the process of improving their financial health and their household finance. The gains may be incremental, but they are real. The gains have been made while preserving as much retirement security and reducing job resignations as possible, while enabling people to access emergency funds. So, I urge the House to support these technical amendments with the same spirit of collaboration that made the original two-part system, as initially proposed by Minister, pass. I thank you. Debate concluded. Question put: That the Revenue Laws Amendment Bill be read the first time. Objections noted. Division called. NOES – 79: (MK – 50; EFF – 28; UAT – 1). The motion is agreed to. Bill accordingly read a first time.
